tips.title: Right-size EC2 instances
tips.description: Use AWS Compute Optimizer to analyze historical utilization and identify underutilized instances. Switch to smaller instance types that match actual workload needs.
tips.automatedCheck: compute-optimizer:GetEC2InstanceRecommendations (ML-based, 14+ days) â†’ PREFERRED over static thresholds. Fallback: cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization + CWAgent/mem_used_percent avg+max 30d â†’ ec2_cpu_metrics. Both CPU AND memory must be low to confirm over-provisioning.

tips.title: Use Reserved Instances or Savings Plans
tips.description: For steady-state workloads, commit to 1-year or 3-year Reserved Instances or Savings Plans. Savings Plans offer more flexibility across instance families and regions.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts(AmazonEC2, actual instance types) â†’ pricing_context shows on-demand vs Savings Plan vs Spot pricing per instance

tips.title: Use Spot Instances for fault-tolerant workloads
tips.description: Spot Instances offer up to 90% discount for interruptible workloads like batch processing, CI/CD, and dev/test environments. Use mixed instance fleets with Auto Scaling.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ ec2_instances list; cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization avg+max 30d; pricing_context.capacity_mix for Spot blend

tips.title: Schedule non-production instances
tips.description: Stop dev/test/staging EC2 instances outside business hours. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate start/stop schedules. A dev environment running only during office hours (10h/day, 5d/week) saves ~70%. Remember: stopped instances still incur EBS storage charges — terminate if data is not needed.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ check tags for Environment=dev/test/staging/qa/sandbox. If non-prod + low CPU â†’ recommend AWS Instance Scheduler to stop nights/weekends (~65% savings). cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization pattern analysis.

tips.title: Implement Auto Scaling
tips.description: Configure Auto Scaling groups with scaling policies based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Use predictive scaling for workloads with predictable patterns.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ check for Auto Scaling group membership; cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization avg vs max spread

tips.title: Use Graviton (ARM) instances
tips.description: AWS Graviton processors offer up to 40% better price-performance than x86 instances. Consider migrating compatible workloads to Graviton-based instance types.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ check instance type family. x86 families (t3, m5, c5, r5, m6i, c6i, r6i) â†’ recommend Graviton equivalents (t4g, m7g, c7g, r7g) for 20-40% better price-performance. ec2_cpu_metrics.graviton_note field.

tips.title: Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering
tips.description: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for data with unknown or changing access patterns. It automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage.
tips.automatedCheck: s3:ListBucketIntelligentTieringConfigurations â†’ s3_optimization_summary.without_intelligent_tiering count. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers. Massive savings for unknown access patterns.

tips.title: Implement lifecycle policies
tips.description: Create lifecycle rules to transition objects to cheaper storage classes (Standard-IA, Glacier) or delete them after a retention period.
tips.automatedCheck: s3:GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration â†’ s3_optimization_summary.without_lifecycle_policy count. Recommend: Standard-IA after 30d, Glacier after 90d, Deep Archive after 180d. Use S3 Storage Lens for visibility.

tips.title: Delete incomplete multipart uploads
tips.description: Incomplete multipart uploads accumulate storage costs. Create a lifecycle rule to abort incomplete uploads after 7 days.
tips.automatedCheck: s3:GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration â†’ check if AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload rule exists per bucket; s3_bucket_analysis[].hasLifecyclePolicy

tips.title: Manage object versions
tips.description: If versioning is enabled, set lifecycle rules to delete old versions or transition them to Glacier. Noncurrent versions can accumulate significant costs.
tips.automatedCheck: s3:GetBucketVersioning â†’ check if versioning enabled; s3:GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration â†’ check for NoncurrentVersionExpiration rule

tips.title: Right-size RDS instances
tips.description: Monitor CPU, memory, and I/O utilization. Downsize over-provisioned instances or switch to burstable (T-class) instances for variable workloads.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(RDS/CPUUtilization+DatabaseConnections+FreeableMemory+ReadIOPS+WriteIOPS, avg+max 30d) â†’ rds_cpu_metrics per instance

tips.title: Use Reserved Instances for RDS
tips.description: For production databases with steady usage, purchase RDS Reserved Instances for 1 or 3 years to reduce costs significantly.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts(AmazonRDS, actual db instance classes, actual engine) â†’ pricing_context with on-demand vs SP vs RI pricing

tips.title: Consider Aurora Serverless v2
tips.description: For variable database workloads, Aurora Serverless v2 scales automatically and charges only for capacity used. Ideal for dev/test and unpredictable traffic.
tips.automatedCheck: rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ rds_instances[].engine; check if engine='aurora-mysql' or 'aurora-postgresql'; cloudwatch:RDS/CPUUtilization avg+max â†’ variable workload = candidate for Serverless v2

tips.title: Optimize RDS storage
tips.description: Use gp3 storage instead of gp2 for better price-performance. Delete old snapshots and reduce allocated storage if over-provisioned.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeVolumes â†’ ebs_summary.gp2_count (RDS uses EBS); rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ storage_gb per instance

tips.title: Right-size Lambda memory
tips.description: Use AWS Lambda Power Tuning to find the optimal memory configuration. More memory means more CPU, which can reduce execution time and cost.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(Lambda/Duration+Invocations, avg+max 30d) â†’ lambda_metrics per function; check memory_mb vs avg_duration_ms

tips.title: Use Graviton for Lambda
tips.description: Configure Lambda functions to use ARM64 architecture (Graviton2). It offers up to 34% better price-performance than x86.
tips.automatedCheck: lambda:ListFunctions â†’ lambda_functions[].Architectures; check if 'x86_64' (not 'arm64') = Graviton migration candidate

tips.title: Avoid unnecessary Provisioned Concurrency
tips.description: Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts but adds cost. Use it only for latency-sensitive production workloads, not dev/test.
tips.automatedCheck: lambda:GetProvisionedConcurrencyConfig per function â†’ check if provisioned concurrency is set; cloudwatch:Lambda/Invocations â†’ low invocations + provisioned = waste

tips.title: Use VPC Endpoints instead of NAT Gateway
tips.description: NAT Gateway charges $0.045/GB for data processing. Use VPC Gateway Endpoints (free for S3/DynamoDB) or Interface Endpoints to reduce costs.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeVpcEndpoints â†’ vpc_endpoints inventory; cloudwatch:NATGateway/BytesOutToDestination â†’ nat_gateway_metrics.total_bytes_out_30d_gb

tips.title: Consolidate NAT Gateways
tips.description: Each NAT Gateway costs ~$32/month plus data processing. Consider using a single NAT Gateway for non-production environments or a NAT instance for low traffic.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeNatGateways â†’ nat_gateway_count; cloudwatch:NATGateway/ActiveConnectionCount â†’ nat_gateway_metrics per gateway

tips.title: Optimize CloudFront cache hit ratio
tips.description: Increase cache TTLs, normalize query strings, and use Origin Shield to improve cache hit ratio. Higher cache hits mean fewer origin requests and lower costs.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(CloudFront/CacheHitRate, avg 30d) â†’ low cache hit rate = optimization needed; ce:GetCostAndUsage for CloudFront cost

tips.title: Use CloudFront Security Savings Bundle
tips.description: If using CloudFront with WAF, the Security Savings Bundle offers up to 30% savings with a 1-year commitment.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage â†’ CloudFront cost; if CloudFront + WAF both in top costs = Security Savings Bundle candidate

tips.title: Use gp3 instead of gp2
tips.description: gp3 volumes are 20% cheaper than gp2 and allow independent IOPS/throughput configuration. Migrate existing gp2 volumes to gp3.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeVolumes â†’ ebs_summary.gp2_count + gp2_gb; gp2_to_gp3_savings_usd = gp2_gb * $0.02

tips.title: Delete unused EBS snapshots
tips.description: Old snapshots accumulate costs. Implement a retention policy and use AWS Backup or Lambda to delete snapshots older than the retention period.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2___other_usage_breakdown â†’ EBS:SnapshotUsage cost line item

tips.title: Use EBS Snapshots Archive
tips.description: For snapshots accessed less than once per quarter, move them to Snapshots Archive tier for up to 75% cost reduction.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2___other_usage_breakdown â†’ EBS:SnapshotUsage cost; if snapshot cost > $10/month = archive candidate for infrequently accessed snapshots

tips.title: Keep traffic within the same region
tips.description: Cross-region data transfer costs $0.02/GB. Co-locate resources in the same region and use regional endpoints to minimize transfer costs.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(usage_type breakdown) â†’ ec2___other_usage_breakdown for DataTransfer-Regional-Bytes cost

tips.title: Use CloudFront for internet egress
tips.description: CloudFront data transfer to internet is cheaper than direct EC2/S3 egress. Use CloudFront even for dynamic content to reduce data transfer costs.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(usage_type breakdown) â†’ DataTransfer-Out-Bytes cost; if egress > $50/month and no CloudFront = candidate

tips.title: Enable AWS Cost Explorer
tips.description: Use Cost Explorer to visualize spending patterns, identify cost drivers, and forecast future costs. Enable hourly granularity for detailed analysis.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage â†’ if API succeeds, Cost Explorer is enabled; cost_by_service data confirms access

tips.title: Set up AWS Budgets with alerts
tips.description: Create budgets with alert thresholds at 50%, 75%, and 100% of expected spend. Use SNS for real-time notifications.
tips.automatedCheck: budgets:DescribeBudgets â†’ check if any budgets exist; 0 budgets = setup recommended

tips.title: Enable Cost Anomaly Detection
tips.description: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to identify unusual spending patterns. Configure alerts to catch unexpected cost spikes early.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetAnomalyMonitors â†’ check if anomaly monitors exist; system also runs its own anomaly detection on daily_cost_trend

tips.title: Audit and remove unused resources
tips.description: Regularly audit for idle resources: unattached EBS volumes, unused Elastic IPs, old snapshots, stopped instances. Use AWS Trusted Advisor for recommendations.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeVolumes(unattached) + ec2:DescribeAddresses(idle) + lambda_metrics(0 invocations) + kms:ListKeys â†’ combined waste detection

tips.title: Implement cost allocation tags
tips.description: Tag all resources with project, environment, and owner tags. Enable cost allocation tags in Billing to track costs by team or application. Use Plan → Tag Resources to scan and bulk-tag resources, then go to Configure → FinOps Settings to activate cost allocation tags.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetTags → check if cost allocation tags are configured; resourcegroupstaggingapi:GetResources → check tag coverage

tips.title: Compute Savings Plans
tips.description: Commit to a consistent $/hr spend for 1 or 3 years. Covers EC2, Lambda, and Fargate across any region, instance family, OS, or tenancy. Most flexible commitment option. Purchase via Cost Explorer > Savings Plans. Choose All Upfront for maximum discount.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts â†’ pricing_context with Compute Savings Plan estimates (~30% discount); ce:GetCostAndUsage for EC2+Lambda+Fargate spend

tips.title: EC2 Instance Savings Plans
tips.description: Commit to a specific EC2 instance family in a chosen region for 1 or 3 years. Deeper discount than Compute SP but less flexible. Applies regardless of instance size, OS, or tenancy within the family. Best for stable, predictable EC2 workloads.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts(AmazonEC2, actual instance types) â†’ pricing_context; ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ actual instance family for SP targeting
tips.actionLabel: Ask AI about EC2 Savings Plans

tips.title: Database Savings Plans
tips.description: Commit to a consistent $/hr spend on database services for 1 or 3 years. Covers Aurora, RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, DocumentDB, Neptune, Keyspaces, Timestream, and DMS. Applies across instance families, sizes, regions, and engines.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts(AmazonRDS, actual db classes) â†’ pricing_context; rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ actual engines for Database SP targeting
tips.actionLabel: Ask AI about Database Savings Plans

tips.title: Standard Reserved Instances
tips.description: Commit to a specific instance type in a specific region for 1 or 3 years. Deepest discount available. Three payment options: All Upfront (max savings), Partial Upfront, No Upfront. Can be resold on RI Marketplace if no longer needed. Best for steady-state workloads that won't change.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts â†’ pricing_context.ri_1yr_per_month for Standard RI pricing comparison vs on-demand

tips.title: Convertible Reserved Instances
tips.description: Like Standard RIs but allow changing instance family, OS, and tenancy during the term. Lower discount than Standard RIs but more flexibility. Cannot be sold on RI Marketplace. Good for workloads that may evolve but will remain on EC2.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts â†’ pricing_context; compare Convertible RI discount vs Compute Savings Plan flexibility

tips.title: Remove idle load balancers
tips.description: Check if your load balancer is idle (fewer than 100 requests/day over 7 days). Each idle ELB costs ~$200/year. Delete unused load balancers or those with no registered/healthy backend instances.
tips.automatedCheck: elbv2:DescribeLoadBalancers + cloudwatch:ALB/RequestCount 30d â†’ elb_metrics.total_requests_30d; 0 requests = idle

tips.title: Right-size load balancer type
tips.description: Evaluate whether you need an Application Load Balancer (ALB), Network Load Balancer (NLB), or Gateway Load Balancer. NLB is cheaper for simple TCP/UDP traffic. Remove unused listeners and target groups.
tips.automatedCheck: elbv2:DescribeLoadBalancers â†’ elb_metrics.type (application/network); cloudwatch:RequestCount for traffic level

tips.title: Audit and remove unused KMS keys
tips.description: Each customer-managed KMS key costs $1/month regardless of usage. Audit keys that have not been used for encryption/decryption in the past 90 days. Disable unused keys and schedule them for deletion. Use AWS-managed keys where possible (free).
tips.automatedCheck: kms:ListKeys + kms:ListAliases â†’ kms_summary.customer_managed_keys count Ã— $1/month

tips.title: Reduce KMS API request costs
tips.description: KMS charges $0.03 per 10,000 API requests. Use data key caching to reduce the number of GenerateDataKey calls. Consider using envelope encryption to minimize direct KMS API calls.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(KMS/NumberOfKeys) â†’ kms_summary; high API request volume in ce:GetCostAndUsage KMS line = caching candidate

tips.title: Check for idle VPC resources
tips.description: Review VPC for idle NAT Gateways, unused Elastic IPs, and empty subnets. NAT Gateways cost $0.045/hr ($32/month) even with zero traffic. Delete idle resources and consider downsizing by consolidating traffic through fewer NAT Gateways.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeNatGateways + ec2:DescribeAddresses + ec2:DescribeVpcEndpoints â†’ nat_gateway_count, elastic_ips.unattached, vpc_endpoints.total; cloudwatch:NATGateway metrics

tips.title: Choose cost-effective AWS regions
tips.description: AWS pricing varies by region. Compare on-demand pricing across regions using the AWS Pricing Calculator. Consider moving non-latency-sensitive workloads to cheaper regions while respecting data sovereignty requirements.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(usage_type breakdown) â†’ usage type prefix reveals region (EU-/USE1-/USW2-); pricing:GetProducts across regions for comparison

tips.title: Optimize Auto Scaling policies for cost
tips.description: Fine-tune ASG scaling policies to avoid over-provisioning. Scale up less aggressively and scale down to the minimum needed. Use predictive scaling for workloads with known patterns and target tracking policies for dynamic loads.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeAutoScalingGroups â†’ check ASG min/max/desired; cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization â†’ if avg CPU low with high max capacity = over-provisioned ASG

tips.title: Use mixed instance fleets with Spot
tips.description: Configure ASGs with mixed instance policies combining On-Demand and Spot instances. Diversify across multiple instance types and AZs to reduce interruption risk while maximizing Spot savings.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ ec2_instances; pricing_context.capacity_mix for Spot blend recommendation; check if mixed instance fleet configured

tips.title: Delete unattached EBS volumes
tips.description: EBS volumes persist after EC2 termination and continue incurring costs. Find volumes with status 'available' using CloudWatch or Trusted Advisor. Enable 'Delete on Termination' for new instances to prevent orphaned volumes.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeVolumes â†’ ebs_summary.unattached_count + unattached_volumes list with volumeId, size_gb, monthly_cost_usd

tips.title: Manage EBS snapshot lifecycle automatically
tips.description: Use Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager to automate snapshot creation and deletion. Set retention policies to avoid accumulating old snapshots. Delete the initial full snapshot when no longer needed as it consumes the most storage.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2___other_usage_breakdown â†’ EBS:SnapshotUsage cost; check if Data Lifecycle Manager policies exist via dlm:GetLifecyclePolicies

tips.title: Use S3 Storage Lens and Analytics
tips.description: Enable S3 Storage Lens for organization-wide visibility into storage usage and activity trends. Use S3 Analytics to identify objects that can be moved to Infrequent Access or Glacier tiers based on actual access patterns.
tips.automatedCheck: s3:ListBuckets + s3:GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration + s3:ListBucketIntelligentTieringConfigurations â†’ s3_optimization_summary. Enable S3 Storage Lens for organization-wide storage analytics and access pattern visibility.

tips.title: Automate resource tagging
tips.description: Embed tags in CloudFormation/Terraform templates. Use AWS Organizations tag policies to enforce tagging rules. Set up Lambda functions to auto-tag resources at launch. Consistent tagging enables accurate cost allocation and orphan detection.
tips.automatedCheck: resourcegroupstaggingapi:GetResources â†’ check tag coverage across resources; low coverage = tagging automation needed

tips.title: Use AWS Cost Optimization Hub
tips.description: Cost Optimization Hub consolidates and prioritizes cost optimization recommendations across all accounts and regions in your AWS Organization. It surfaces rightsizing, idle resource, and commitment recommendations in one place.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage + compute-optimizer:GetEC2InstanceRecommendations â†’ system already consolidates these; tip is advisory

tips.title: Implement chargeback with billing groups
tips.description: Use AWS Enterprise Billing Console to allocate costs to departments or business units. Create billing groups with customized pricing plans and CUR reports per group to drive accountability and identify optimization opportunities per team.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage â†’ cost_by_service breakdown; if multi-team account with no tag-based allocation = chargeback candidate

tips.title: Sell unused Reserved Instances on RI Marketplace
tips.description: If you have Standard RIs you no longer need, list them on the AWS RI Marketplace to recoup costs. For future purchases, consider Convertible RIs if workloads may change, or Standard RIs if you want the resale option.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts â†’ pricing_context; if RI pricing shown but workload changed = RI Marketplace sell candidate

tips.title: Use CloudFront to reduce load balancer data transfer
tips.description: Place CloudFront in front of your ALB to cache responses and reduce data transfer through the load balancer. CloudFront data transfer is cheaper than direct ALB egress and reduces origin load.
tips.automatedCheck: elbv2:DescribeLoadBalancers + cloudwatch:ALB/RequestCount â†’ elb_metrics; ce:GetCostAndUsage â†’ DataTransfer cost through ALB; if high = CloudFront candidate

tips.title: Right-size ElastiCache nodes
tips.description: Monitor memory utilization, CPU, and network metrics. Downsize over-provisioned cache nodes. Consider using ElastiCache Serverless for variable workloads to pay only for data stored and compute used.
tips.automatedCheck: elasticache:DescribeCacheClusters â†’ node types; cloudwatch:ElastiCache/CPUUtilization+FreeableMemory+CurrConnections avg+max 30d â†’ rightsizing

tips.title: Use Reserved Nodes for ElastiCache
tips.description: For steady-state caching workloads, purchase ElastiCache Reserved Nodes for 1 or 3 years. Covered by Database Savings Plans as well.
tips.automatedCheck: pricing:GetProducts(AmazonElastiCache) â†’ on-demand vs reserved pricing; elasticache:DescribeReservedCacheNodes â†’ check existing reservations

tips.title: Switch to On-Demand capacity for variable workloads
tips.description: If your DynamoDB tables have unpredictable traffic, use On-Demand capacity mode to avoid paying for provisioned capacity you don't use. For steady workloads, provisioned mode with auto-scaling is cheaper.
tips.automatedCheck: dynamodb:DescribeTable â†’ BillingMode (PROVISIONED vs PAY_PER_REQUEST); cloudwatch:DynamoDB/ConsumedReadCapacityUnits+ConsumedWriteCapacityUnits â†’ variable = On-Demand candidate

tips.title: Use DynamoDB Reserved Capacity
tips.description: For predictable DynamoDB workloads, purchase reserved capacity for 1 or 3 years. Also covered by Database Savings Plans for more flexibility across database services.
tips.automatedCheck: dynamodb:DescribeTable â†’ BillingMode=PROVISIONED; cloudwatch:DynamoDB/ConsumedReadCapacityUnits avg+max â†’ steady = reserved capacity candidate

tips.title: Match instance family to workload type
tips.description: Use C-family instances for CPU-intensive workloads instead of general-purpose M-family. Use T-family burstable instances for workloads with periodic CPU spikes. Use AWS Compute Optimizer to analyze running workloads and get instance type recommendations.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ instance type family (t3=burstable, m5=general, c5=compute); cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization pattern â†’ match family to workload

tips.title: Use AWS Instance Scheduler for non-production
tips.description: Schedule GPU and compute-intensive instances to run only during work hours. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate stop/start and save up to 70% on expensive instance types.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ check tags for Environment=dev/test/staging. If running 24/7 â†’ recommend AWS Instance Scheduler. ec2_cpu_metrics.environment_tag field. Office hours (10h/day, 5d/week) saves ~70%.

tips.title: Enable EC2 hibernation for infrequently used instances
tips.description: Instead of stopping instances, hibernate them to preserve in-memory state. This avoids cold-start delays when resuming while still saving compute costs during idle periods.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ check instance state; cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization â†’ long idle periods = hibernation candidate

tips.title: Use Availability Zone affinity to reduce cross-AZ costs
tips.description: Cross-AZ data transfer costs $0.01/GB each way. Use AZ affinity routing to keep traffic within the same AZ when possible. Deploy read replicas in each AZ so reads stay local. Balance this with multi-AZ resilience requirements.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(usage_type breakdown) â†’ DataTransfer-Regional-Bytes cost in ec2___other_usage_breakdown

tips.title: Compress data before transfer
tips.description: Compress data before transferring between regions, AZs, or to the internet. Use gzip or zstd compression for API responses, log shipping, and backup transfers to reduce volume and associated costs.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(usage_type breakdown) â†’ DataTransfer-Out-Bytes + DataTransfer-Regional-Bytes total; high volume = compression candidate

tips.title: Use read replicas to offload database queries
tips.description: Create RDS read replicas to offload read-heavy queries from the primary instance. This frees capacity on the primary node and can delay or avoid costly instance upsizing. Place replicas in the same AZ as your application for lower latency and no cross-AZ transfer costs.
tips.automatedCheck: rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ rds_instances[].engine, class; cloudwatch:RDS/ReadIOPS avg+max 30d â†’ high read IOPS = read replica candidate

tips.title: Migrate from commercial to open-source databases
tips.description: Migrate from licensed databases (Oracle, SQL Server) to open-source alternatives (PostgreSQL, MySQL) on RDS or Aurora. Eliminates license costs and Aurora offers better price-performance. Use AWS SCT and DMS for migration.
tips.automatedCheck: rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ rds_instances[].engine; engine='oracle-ee'|'sqlserver-ee'|'sqlserver-se' = migration candidate to PostgreSQL/MySQL

tips.title: Migrate to serverless where possible
tips.description: Replace always-on EC2 instances with Lambda, Fargate, or Aurora Serverless for variable workloads. Serverless services scale to zero when idle, eliminating costs during low-traffic periods. Ideal for APIs, event processing, and dev/test environments.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ low-utilization instances; cloudwatch:EC2/CPUUtilization avg < 10% + lambda_metrics â†’ if workload is event-driven = serverless candidate

tips.title: Use Amazon Lightsail for simple, predictable workloads
tips.description: For simple websites, blogs, or small applications, Amazon Lightsail offers fixed monthly pricing starting at $3.50/month including compute, storage, and data transfer. Avoids unpredictable billing from multiple AWS services.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances â†’ single small instance running web server; ce:GetCostAndUsage total < $50/month = Lightsail candidate

tips.title: Set up billing alarms from day one
tips.description: Create CloudWatch billing alarms for estimated charges immediately when setting up a new AWS account. Set alerts at multiple thresholds (50%, 75%, 100% of expected spend). This catches unexpected cost spikes before they become expensive surprises.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:DescribeAlarms(AlarmNamePrefix='Billing') â†’ check if billing alarms exist; 0 alarms = setup recommended

tips.title: Engage your AWS account manager
tips.description: AWS account managers provide personalized cost-saving recommendations, guidance on pricing models, and access to funding programs for startups. They can connect you with AWS experts and training resources. Reach out regularly for tailored optimization advice.
tips.automatedCheck: Advisory: no API check â€” recommend engaging AWS account manager for accounts with > $10K/month spend based on cost_by_service total

tips.title: Monitor and optimize EFS throughput settings
tips.description: Review EFS storage usage and switch to Bursting throughput mode if your workload has variable I/O patterns. Use EFS Infrequent Access storage class for files not accessed for 30+ days. Enable lifecycle management to auto-transition files.
tips.automatedCheck: efs:DescribeFileSystems â†’ check ThroughputMode (bursting/provisioned); cloudwatch:EFS/TotalIOBytes avg+max â†’ low I/O with provisioned throughput = switch to bursting

tips.title: Right-size ECS task definitions
tips.description: Monitor CPU and memory utilization per ECS service. Over-provisioned resource requests (CPU/memory limits set too high) are the most common container waste pattern. Reduce task CPU/memory to match actual usage.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(ECS/CPUUtilization+MemoryUtilization per service, avg+max 30d) â†’ ecs_service_metrics. avg CPU < 10% AND avg memory < 20% = OVER-PROVISIONED task definitions.

tips.title: Enable ECS Service Auto Scaling
tips.description: Configure target tracking scaling policies for ECS services based on CPU or memory utilization. Scale down during low-traffic periods to reduce running task count and associated costs.
tips.automatedCheck: ecs:DescribeServices â†’ check if scalableTarget exists. ecs_service_metrics.avg_cpu_pct < 30% with fixed desiredCount = auto-scaling candidate.

tips.title: Right-size EKS node groups and pod resource requests
tips.description: Kubernetes nodes often run at 10-20% capacity due to overly conservative pod resource requests. Use Kubernetes Metrics Server or Container Insights to analyze actual pod CPU/memory usage and reduce resource requests to match.
tips.automatedCheck: eks:ListClusters + eks:DescribeCluster â†’ eks_clusters inventory. cloudwatch:ContainerInsights/node_cpu_utilization + node_memory_utilization for node-level rightsizing.

tips.title: Enable Karpenter or Cluster Autoscaler for EKS
tips.description: Use Karpenter (recommended) or Cluster Autoscaler to automatically provision right-sized nodes based on pending pod requirements. Karpenter can select optimal instance types and use Spot instances for significant savings.
tips.automatedCheck: eks:ListNodegroups â†’ check if managed node groups have auto-scaling enabled. eks_clusters with fixed node count = autoscaler candidate.

tips.title: Schedule non-production RDS & Aurora databases
tips.description: Stop non-production RDS instances outside business hours. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate stop/start schedules for dev/test databases. Stopped RDS instances auto-restart after 7 days — the scheduler handles re-stopping automatically.
tips.automatedCheck: rds:DescribeDBInstances â†’ check tags for Environment=dev/test/staging. If running 24/7 with low connections â†’ recommend stop schedule. rds_cpu_metrics + rds_conn_14d for idle detection.

tips.title: Scale Auto Scaling Groups to zero after hours
tips.description: For development environments using Auto Scaling Groups, update the desired, minimum, and maximum capacity to 0 at night and reset them in the morning. This eliminates all compute costs during idle periods while preserving the ASG configuration.
tips.automatedCheck: autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingGroups â†’ check tags for Environment=dev/test. If running 24/7 with non-prod tags â†’ recommend scaling to 0 after hours.

tips.title: Scale EKS node groups to zero after hours
tips.description: Scale EKS node groups to zero outside business hours for non-production clusters. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate scale-down/restore schedules.
tips.automatedCheck: eks:ListNodegroups + eks:DescribeNodegroup â†’ check scaling config. If min > 0 and non-prod tags â†’ recommend scaling to 0 after hours.

tips.title: Stop idle SageMaker notebook instances
tips.description: Stop SageMaker notebook instances when not in use. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate stop/start schedules for notebooks used only during business hours.
tips.automatedCheck: sagemaker:ListNotebookInstances â†’ check status. If InService with no recent activity â†’ recommend stop or auto-stop lifecycle config.

tips.title: Pause non-production Redshift clusters
tips.description: Pause Redshift clusters outside business hours for dev/test workloads. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate pause/resume schedules.
tips.automatedCheck: redshift:DescribeClusters â†’ check tags for Environment=dev/test. If running 24/7 â†’ recommend pause schedule.

tips.title: Enable auto-stop mode for WorkSpaces
tips.description: Switch WorkSpaces to AUTO_STOP running mode for users who do not need always-on desktops. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate the switch.
tips.automatedCheck: workspaces:DescribeWorkspaces â†’ check RunningMode. If ALWAYS_ON â†’ recommend AUTO_STOP for non-critical users.

tips.title: Schedule ASG scale-down outside business hours
tips.description: Scale Auto Scaling Groups to zero outside business hours for non-production environments. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automatically set desired/min/max to 0 at night and restore original values in the morning.
tips.automatedCheck: autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingGroups → check tags for Environment=dev/test/staging. If non-prod + running 24/7 → recommend scheduling.

tips.title: Tear down non-production load balancers after hours
tips.description: Each ALB costs ~$16/month minimum even with zero traffic. Use SlashMyBill Scheduler (Act → Scheduler) to automate teardown of non-production load balancers outside business hours.
tips.automatedCheck: elbv2:DescribeLoadBalancers → check tags for Environment=dev/test/staging. If non-prod ALB → recommend scheduling teardown.

tips.title: Activate Cost Allocation Tags
tips.description: Enable user-defined cost allocation tags (Environment, Owner, CostCenter, Application) to categorize costs in billing reports. Without active tags, cost attribution is limited to service-level breakdowns.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:ListCostAllocationTags(Type=UserDefined) → check active vs inactive count

tips.title: Activate AWS-Generated Tags
tips.description: Enable the aws:createdBy tag to automatically track which IAM user or role created each resource. This helps with accountability and cost attribution without manual tagging.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:ListCostAllocationTags(Type=AWSGenerated) → check aws:createdBy status

tips.title: Set Up Cost Anomaly Detection
tips.description: Configure AWS Cost Anomaly Detection monitors to receive alerts when spending deviates from expected patterns. Monitors use ML to detect unusual cost spikes by service.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetAnomalyMonitors → check if at least 1 monitor exists

tips.title: Enable Hourly Cost Granularity
tips.description: Enable hourly cost data in Cost Explorer for real-time cost visibility. This allows detection of cost spikes within hours instead of waiting for daily aggregation.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetCostAndUsage(Granularity=HOURLY) → probe if hourly data is available

tips.title: Enable EC2 Rightsizing Recommendations
tips.description: Enable Cost Explorer rightsizing recommendations to receive suggestions for downsizing or terminating underutilized EC2 instances based on CloudWatch metrics.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:GetPreferences → check RightsizingRecommendations enabled

tips.title: Configure Cost and Usage Report (CUR)
tips.description: Set up a Cost and Usage Report to export detailed billing data to S3. CUR provides the most granular cost data available, enabling advanced analytics and custom dashboards.
tips.automatedCheck: cur:DescribeReportDefinitions → check if at least 1 report exists

tips.title: Run Tag Backfill for Historical Data
tips.description: Start a cost allocation tag backfill to retroactively apply tag data to up to 12 months of historical billing records. This enables tag-based cost analysis on past spending.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:ListCostAllocationTagBackfillHistory → check for completed backfill

tips.title: Enable Linked Account Billing Access
tips.description: Allow linked accounts in your AWS Organization to access their own billing data. This enables team members in linked accounts to view costs and create budgets.
tips.automatedCheck: organizations:DescribeOrganization → check linked account billing access settings

tips.title: Set Up AWS Budgets with Alerts
tips.description: Create at least one AWS Budget with cost alerts at 50%, 75%, and 100% thresholds. Budgets provide proactive spending notifications before costs exceed expectations.
tips.automatedCheck: budgets:DescribeBudgets → check if at least 1 budget exists

tips.title: Improve Resource Tag Coverage
tips.description: Ensure at least 80% of your resources are tagged with standard tags (Environment, Owner, CostCenter, Application). High tag coverage enables accurate cost attribution and showback.
tips.automatedCheck: tag:GetResources → compute tagged/total resource ratio

tips.title: Enroll in AWS Compute Optimizer
tips.description: Enroll in AWS Compute Optimizer to receive ML-based rightsizing recommendations for EC2, EBS, Lambda, and ECS. Compute Optimizer analyzes 14+ days of CloudWatch metrics.
tips.automatedCheck: compute-optimizer:GetEnrollmentStatus → check if status is Active

tips.title: Check Tag Activation Status (Linked Account)
tips.description: Verify that cost allocation tags have been activated by your management account admin. Linked accounts cannot activate tags themselves but can check the current status.
tips.automatedCheck: ce:ListCostAllocationTags → read-only check of tag activation status

tips.title: Optimize ASG clusters with Spot mix and multi-AZ
tips.description: Use the Optimize a Cluster wizard (Act > Optimize) to analyze your Auto Scaling Groups. The wizard checks 7 best practices: multi-AZ deployment, load balancer attachment, Spot Instance mix with price-capacity-optimized strategy, instance type diversification, scaling policies, Launch Template usage, and ELB health checks. Each check includes a specific fix recommendation.
tips.automatedCheck: autoscaling:DescribeAutoScalingGroups + DescribePolicies + elbv2:DescribeTargetHealth -> cluster health score

tips.title: Resize over-provisioned EC2 instances
tips.description: Use the Resize a Server wizard (Act > Optimize) to analyze 30 days of CPU and memory usage, then resize to a cheaper instance type with one click. The wizard shows full instance specs (vCPU, memory, network, EBS IOPS, architecture) and a sortable comparison table of alternatives sorted by cost. Supports automatic stop-modify-start with downtime warning.
tips.automatedCheck: cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics(CPUUtilization, mem_used_percent) 30d + ec2:DescribeInstanceTypes + pricing:GetProducts -> rightsizing table

tips.title: Optimize Windows Server + SQL Server vCPU licensing costs
tips.description: Windows Server and SQL Server are licensed per vCPU/core. Use the EC2 Optimize CPUs feature to specify a custom number of active vCPUs — this lets you keep high memory and IOPS for database performance while reducing the vCPU count to lower licensing costs. For example, disabling hyperthreading halves the vCPU count and the associated license fees. Switch to memory-optimized instances (R5, R6i, R7i) that provide higher memory-to-vCPU ratios — SQL Server is typically memory-hungry but CPU-light.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances -> platform='windows' + ec2:DescribeInstanceTypes -> vCpuInfo.DefaultCores vs vCpuInfo.DefaultVCpus; cloudwatch:CPUUtilization avg<30% = candidate for vCPU reduction

tips.title: Use BYOL or Dedicated Hosts for Windows/SQL Server licensing
tips.description: Compare License Included (LI) vs Bring Your Own License (BYOL) for Windows and SQL Server workloads. BYOL with Software Assurance can save up to 77% on Windows Server and 45% on SQL Server costs. For older licenses without Software Assurance, use EC2 Dedicated Hosts to license at the physical core level rather than vCPU — this is more cost-effective for dense workloads. For HA setups, AWS provides cost reductions for passive SQL Server nodes where a second license may not be required.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances -> platform='windows'; pricing:GetProducts(AmazonEC2, operatingSystem=Windows, licenseModel=*) -> compare LI vs BYOL pricing per instance type

tips.title: Request AWS Optimization and Licensing Assessment (OLA)
tips.description: AWS offers a no-cost Optimization and Licensing Assessment (OLA) that evaluates your actual Windows/SQL Server workload usage (CPU, memory, storage, IOPS) and provides rightsized instance recommendations plus a business case for licensing optimization. The OLA can identify opportunities to downgrade SQL Server from Enterprise to Standard edition if Enterprise features are not fully utilized, saving significant license costs. Use AWS Compute Optimizer for automated ML-based recommendations on over-provisioned SQL Server instances.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeInstances -> platform='windows' + tags containing 'sql' or 'mssql'; compute-optimizer:GetEC2InstanceRecommendations -> check if recommendation suggests smaller instance

tips.title: Optimize RDS SQL Server licensing and instance sizing
tips.description: RDS SQL Server instances are licensed per vCPU. Rightsize by switching to memory-optimized instance classes (db.r5, db.r6i) that provide more memory per vCPU — SQL Server benefits from high memory for buffer pool caching. Consider downgrading from SQL Server Enterprise to Standard edition if you don't use Enterprise-only features (partitioning, compression, Always On AG with multiple secondaries). For read-heavy workloads, use read replicas instead of scaling up the primary. Track licenses with AWS License Manager to avoid over-provisioning.
tips.automatedCheck: rds:DescribeDBInstances -> engine='sqlserver-ee'|'sqlserver-se' + DBInstanceClass; cloudwatch:RDS/CPUUtilization avg<30% = over-provisioned; check if Enterprise features used to determine downgrade eligibility

tips.title: Set CloudWatch Logs retention periods
tips.description: CloudWatch Logs default to Never Expire, accumulating storage costs indefinitely at $0.03/GB/month. Set retention policies: 7 days for dev/test, 30 days for staging, 90 days for production. Export critical logs to S3 (cheaper) before they expire. A single busy Lambda function can generate GBs of logs monthly.
tips.automatedCheck: logs:DescribeLogGroups -> check retentionInDays; null = Never Expire = waste. Script: aws logs put-retention-policy --log-group-name /aws/lambda/my-func --retention-in-days 30
tips.script: aws logs describe-log-groups --query 'logGroups[?!retentionInDays].logGroupName' --output text | while read lg; do aws logs put-retention-policy --log-group-name "$lg" --retention-in-days 30; done

tips.title: Delete empty and unused log groups
tips.description: Old Lambda functions, deleted ECS tasks, and removed services leave behind empty log groups. Each log group with stored data costs money. Delete log groups with 0 stored bytes or no log events in 90+ days.
tips.automatedCheck: logs:DescribeLogGroups -> storedBytes=0 or no recent events; Script: aws logs describe-log-groups --query 'logGroups[?storedBytes==`0`].logGroupName'
tips.script: aws logs describe-log-groups --query 'logGroups[?storedBytes==`0`].logGroupName' --output text | while read lg; do echo "Deleting empty log group: $lg"; aws logs delete-log-group --log-group-name "$lg"; done

tips.title: Release unassociated Elastic IPs
tips.description: Since February 2024, ALL Elastic IPs cost $3.75/month ($0.005/hr) whether attached or not. Previously only unattached EIPs were charged. Review all EIPs and release any that are not actively needed. Each unused EIP wastes $45/year.
tips.automatedCheck: ec2:DescribeAddresses -> filter AssociationId=null for unattached; all EIPs now cost money. Script: aws ec2 describe-addresses --query 'Addresses[?!AssociationId].AllocationId'

tips.title: Optimize Rekognition API call volume
tips.description: Rekognition charges per image/video analyzed ($1-$12 per 1000 images depending on API). Reduce costs by: caching results for duplicate images, using lower resolution for initial screening, batching face comparisons with IndexFaces instead of individual CompareFaces calls, and deleting unused collections.
tips.automatedCheck: rekognition:ListCollections -> count collections; cloudwatch:Rekognition/SuccessfulRequestCount -> track API volume; ce:GetCostAndUsage(Rekognition) -> monthly cost trend
tips.script: aws rekognition list-collections --query 'CollectionIds' --output table && aws ce get-cost-and-usage --time-period Start=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) --granularity MONTHLY --filter '{"Dimensions":{"Key":"SERVICE","Values":["Amazon Rekognition"]}}' --metrics UnblendedCost --query 'ResultsByTime[].Total.UnblendedCost.Amount'

tips.title: Delete unused Rekognition collections
tips.description: Rekognition collections store face vectors at $0.01 per 1000 face metadata stored per month. Old collections from testing or decommissioned features accumulate costs. List and delete collections no longer in use.
tips.automatedCheck: rekognition:ListCollections + DescribeCollection -> FaceCount per collection; delete collections with 0 faces or unused ones
tips.script: aws rekognition list-collections --output text | while read coll; do count=$(aws rekognition describe-collection --collection-id "$coll" --query 'FaceCount'); echo "$coll: $count faces"; done

tips.title: Use smaller/cheaper Bedrock models for simple tasks
tips.description: Not every AI task needs the most powerful model. Use Claude Haiku or Nova Lite ($0.25/M input tokens) for classification, summarization, and simple Q&A instead of Claude Sonnet ($3/M tokens) or Opus ($15/M tokens). Route complex tasks to larger models only when needed. Implement prompt caching to reduce repeated token costs.
tips.automatedCheck: bedrock:ListModelInvocationLoggingConfigurations; cloudwatch:Bedrock/Invocations per model; ce:GetCostAndUsage(Bedrock) -> cost trend. Compare model usage distribution.
tips.script: aws ce get-cost-and-usage --time-period Start=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d),End=$(date +%Y-%m-%d) --granularity DAILY --filter '{"Dimensions":{"Key":"SERVICE","Values":["Amazon Bedrock"]}}' --metrics UnblendedCost --query 'ResultsByTime[].[TimePeriod.Start,Total.UnblendedCost.Amount]' --output table

tips.title: Use Provisioned Throughput for high-volume Bedrock
tips.description: If you make consistent high-volume Bedrock API calls (>1000/day), Provisioned Throughput offers a fixed hourly rate regardless of token count. Compare your on-demand token costs vs provisioned pricing. For batch processing, use Batch Inference (50% cheaper than real-time).
tips.automatedCheck: bedrock:ListProvisionedModelThroughputs; ce:GetCostAndUsage(Bedrock) -> if monthly cost > $500, evaluate provisioned throughput

tips.title: Delete unused Amplify apps and branches
tips.description: Amplify charges for build minutes ($0.01/min), hosting ($0.15/GB served), and stored artifacts. Old preview branches, abandoned apps, and unused backends accumulate costs. Delete apps no longer in use and prune old branches.
tips.automatedCheck: amplify:ListApps -> list all apps; amplify:ListBranches per app; check for apps with no recent deployments (>90 days)

tips.title: Optimize SES sending costs
tips.description: SES charges $0.10 per 1000 emails sent. Reduce costs by: cleaning your mailing list (remove bounces/complaints), using suppression lists, batching sends with SES v2 bulk API, and avoiding unnecessary transactional emails. Monitor bounce rates — high bounces waste money and hurt deliverability.
tips.automatedCheck: ses:GetSendStatistics -> bounce rate, complaint rate; ses:GetAccount -> sending limits; high bounce rate = list cleanup needed
